definition: An AI agent is software that perceives its environment, decides an action, and executes it — autonomously or with human approval. Unlike a chatbot that only responds, an agent acts on the world (sends emails, updates databases, calls APIs, schedules meetings).
longExplanation: Modern AI agents are typically built on a frontier LLM (frontier LLMs) plus a tool-calling layer that lets the LLM execute real-world actions. Each agent has a system prompt defining its role, access to specific tools, and a memory layer. The Aiprosol consultancy is operationally run by 10 AI agents — Arora (CEO), COO, CMO, CCO, CTO, CRO, CLO, CPO, CPM, and DA — each scoped to a domain and reporting to one human Chairman.

definition: AI automation combines workflow orchestration (Zapier / Make / n8n) with AI judgement (LLMs for classification, extraction, drafting) to handle business tasks that previously required human attention. The defining trait: AI doesn't just trigger an action, it makes a judgement call inside it.
longExplanation: Pure rule-based automation: 'when a new lead arrives, send a welcome email.' AI-augmented automation: 'when a new lead arrives, classify their intent, score their fit, draft a personalised reply, and send only if confidence > 80%.' The AI layer adds the judgement step. AI automation is the core service Aiprosol designs and operates for clients.

definition: Workflow automation chains tools together so events in one system trigger actions in another — without human glue work. Classic example: Stripe charge → CRM updated → welcome email sent → onboarding folder created. Tools: Zapier, Make, n8n, custom code.
longExplanation: Workflow automation predates AI automation by ~15 years (Zapier launched 2011). The unlock is removing manual data entry and notification work between SaaS tools. Most SMBs reclaim 10-20 hrs/week with pure workflow automation before adding any AI. See the Workflow Automation Playbook for the 7 core patterns + 5 anti-patterns.

definition: A large language model is a neural network trained on vast text data that can generate human-like text, answer questions, classify content, extract structured data, and call external tools. The current frontier models are frontier LLMs from leading AI labs.
longExplanation: LLMs are the engine behind chatbots, frontier LLM chat interfaces, and most AI features in modern SaaS. For business automation: Frontier LLMs excel at long-context accuracy + tool use, a budget LLM wins on bulk classification at low cost, an alternative frontier LLM wins on native Google Workspace integration. Aiprosol's 10-agent C-suite runs on a frontier LLM with an open-source fallback.

definition: RAG is the pattern of giving an LLM access to your own data at runtime — fetching relevant context from a vector database, embedding it in the prompt, and letting the LLM generate an answer grounded in that data. Used for: customer support over your knowledge base, internal Q&A, document analysis.
longExplanation: Without RAG, LLMs answer from training data only — which is generic and stale. With RAG, you can ask 'what's our refund policy?' and get an answer grounded in your actual policy doc. Aiprosol's chat widget uses RAG against Supabase pgvector. Tools: pgvector (best for SMB), Pinecone (scale), Weaviate (hybrid search), LangChain/LlamaIndex (orchestration).

definition: A vector database stores text-as-embeddings (high-dimensional numeric arrays representing meaning) and supports similarity search — 'find documents most similar to this query.' The backbone of RAG and AI search. Top picks: pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate.
longExplanation: Embeddings are generated by an embedding model (leading embedding-model APIs). Each chunk of text becomes a ~1500-dimensional vector. The DB indexes these for nearest-neighbour search. For SMB scale (under 100k documents), pgvector (Postgres extension) is plenty. Pinecone is overkill for most businesses and adds a separate vendor relationship.

definition: n8n is an open-source workflow automation tool (fair-code licensed) similar to Zapier and Make, but self-hostable and free of per-execution pricing. Strong for technical teams wanting full control + unmetered runs.
longExplanation: n8n's defining feature: you self-host it on a $5/month VPS and run unlimited workflows. Compare to Zapier where each step in a workflow uses a metered 'task' charged at ~$0.02 each. At 10k+ runs/day, n8n self-hosted costs <1% of Zapier. Aiprosol standardises on n8n for engineering-led teams. All 25 starter workflows in our product library are n8n exports.

definition: Zapier is the largest workflow automation platform (5,000+ app integrations). Workflows are called 'Zaps' — a trigger event in one app fires actions in others. Hosted SaaS, easy for non-technical users, expensive at scale.
longExplanation: Zapier launched in 2011 and pioneered the no-code automation category. Its breadth (5,000+ apps integrated) is unmatched. Pricing: per-task model means workflows running thousands of times/day get expensive fast. Sweet spot: 1-15 Zaps under 100 runs/day. Beyond that, Make or n8n become cheaper. Aiprosol's Zapier + Make Power User Bundle ($147) documents 25 Zapier recipes you can build in your own account.

definition: Make.com is a visual workflow automation platform — Zapier's main competitor. Strongest at branching, iterating, and aggregating data. Cheaper per-operation than Zapier; less app coverage. Excellent fit for ops teams comfortable with visual programming.
longExplanation: Make rebranded from Integromat in 2022. Its differentiation: visual flow editor showing data transformations + iterators/aggregators as first-class concepts. Make typically wins on cost-per-operation vs. Zapier above ~2,000 runs/month. Aiprosol's Zapier + Make Power User Bundle includes 25 detailed Make recipes alongside the Zapier ones.

definition: Return on investment for AI automation = (weekly hours reclaimed × fully-loaded hourly cost × annual weeks) ÷ (tool cost + implementation cost). Aiprosol's benchmark across clients: 35+ hours/week reclaimed, 340% average annual ROI.
longExplanation: The honest formula: count the manual hours someone spent per week BEFORE automation, count after, multiply the delta by their fully-loaded hourly cost ($50-200 for ops/sales/CS staff), multiply by 50 working weeks. Subtract tool subscriptions ($200-2,000/mo typical) and implementation cost (DIY: $0 + 80 hours; consultant: $5-25K). Most automation projects pay back in <8 weeks. See aiprosol.com/how-we-measure for the methodology behind our 35-hour claim.

definition: Hours reclaimed = weekly time a person used to spend on manual work that's now automated. Measured BEFORE and AFTER engagement to keep the number honest. Aiprosol's 90-day reclaim guarantee: 35+ hours/week or we work for free until you hit it.
longExplanation: Reclaimed hours go back into the business as either capacity to grow (taking on more customers / products) or cost reduction (deferring hires). The 35-hour benchmark splits roughly: Sales 8h/wk, Customer Success 12h/wk, Operations 10h/wk, Finance 5h/wk. Different industries skew differently — Legal reclaims more on document automation, E-commerce more on customer support.

definition: A managed plan is an ongoing service where Aiprosol designs, builds, AND operates your AI automation systems — not just builds-and-disappears. Three tiers: Starter ($997/mo · 5-15 person businesses), Growth ($2,997/mo · 15-50), Enterprise ($7,997/mo · 50-5,000).
longExplanation: The difference vs. project-based consulting: managed plans include weekly metric reviews, monthly optimisation cycles, quarterly roadmap, and a 90-day reclaim guarantee. The customer never owns the operational burden. If a workflow breaks, we fix it. If a tool changes its API, we adapt. If a better tool emerges, we migrate. Compare with one-shot consulting where the customer assumes maintenance from day 31.

definition: An automation audit is the 60-90 minute discovery process of mapping a business's recurring manual work, scoring each task on impact × ease, and producing a prioritised list of automation candidates. Aiprosol's free 60-second ROI Audit at /roi-audit produces an initial scorecard; the full audit happens in week 1 of any managed plan.
longExplanation: The 5-step framework: (1) catalogue every recurring task by frequency × duration, (2) triage into 4 buckets — automate now / augment with AI / human-only / eliminate, (3) score automate-candidates on impact (hours × cost × 12) ÷ ease (build hours), (4) decide tool per task using the Zapier vs Make vs n8n grid, (5) ship one workflow per week for the next 12 weeks. Full framework in the Business Process Audit Checklist ($37).

definition: Lead scoring assigns a 0-100 score to each inbound lead based on fit + intent + engagement + urgency. High-scoring leads route to fast-response queues; low-scoring leads enter nurture sequences. Modern lead scoring blends rule-based logic with AI judgement on free-text fields.
longExplanation: Aiprosol's 4-component model: FIT (40 points) — company size + revenue + industry + decision-maker title. INTENT (30 points) — form type + manual hours reported + free-text length. ENGAGEMENT (20 points) — multi-page visit + pricing page + return visit + email engagement. URGENCY (10 points) — time-based keywords + competitor mentions. Routing thresholds: 85+ Hot (5-min SLA), 65-84 Warm (4-hour SLA), 40-64 Nurture (auto-sequence), 0-39 Future (archive).

definition: Responding to inbound leads within 3 minutes yields 21x more qualifications than responding within 30 minutes. Most B2B companies take >24 hours. Sub-3-minute response is a learned discipline backed by automation: lead form → enrich → score → instant acknowledgment + Slack ping to AE.
longExplanation: The data is brutal: respond in 3 min → 21x conversion lift. 5 min → 9x. 30 min → baseline. 24+ hrs → competitor wins. The architecture: form submission posts directly to a serverless function (not async CRM), enrichment is fire-and-forget if >15s, scoring takes 1s, decision branch fires within 60-90s. Aiprosol's Lead Generation Automation Playbook ($127) ships the full system blueprint + 4 n8n workflow starters.

definition: A lead nurture sequence is a multi-touch email cadence that warms inbound leads who didn't convert immediately. The Aiprosol 5-touch sequence: Touch 1 (immediate · personalised ROI estimate), Touch 2 (Day 3 · peer case study), Touch 3 (Day 7 · objection killer), Touch 4 (Day 14 · permission check-in), Touch 5 (Day 21 · low-stakes offer).
longExplanation: The discipline is conversion-per-touch — every email earns the next read. Day 14's permission check-in matters more than people expect: it filters cold subscribers AND signals respect. Aiprosol's Cold Outreach Library bundles 8 specific patterns (peer reference, trigger event, mutual connection, PS-only breakup, etc.) with reply-rate baselines.

definition: Cold outreach is reaching prospects who haven't requested contact. Done well it drives 6-12% reply rates; done badly it spams. The 8 patterns that work: 30-second value claim, competitor-switch, peer-reference, trigger-event, mutual-connection, contrarian-opinion, pure-question, PS-only.
longExplanation: The Cold Outreach Library bundled with Aiprosol's Lead Generation Playbook documents each pattern with subject A/B, body copy, personalisation token map, and historical reply-rate baselines. Worst pattern: 'I hope this email finds you well.' Best pattern (highest acceptance): mutual-connection — but only with permission to use the connection's name.

definition: MRR = predictable monthly revenue from subscriptions. The key SaaS metric. New MRR = new subs this month × ARPU. Net New MRR = New + Expansion − Churn − Contraction. Used to forecast cash + measure growth velocity.
longExplanation: MRR matters because SaaS businesses are valued at multiples of ARR (=MRR × 12). For consultancies on managed plans, MRR works the same way: each customer's monthly fee accrues to MRR; lost customers shrink it. Aiprosol's managed plan structure ($997 / $2,997 / $7,997) is designed for predictable MRR — one Enterprise customer = ~$96K ARR.

definition: Churn = % of customers (or MRR) you lose in a given period. Logo churn = customer-count basis. Revenue churn = MRR basis. SMB SaaS benchmark: 3-7% monthly logo churn (healthy), 8%+ is a problem. Aiprosol's managed-plan churn guarantee: 90-day reclaim or we work for free.
longExplanation: Churn is the single biggest lever for SaaS / managed-service economics. A 3% monthly churn rate equals 30% annual customer loss. Reducing churn from 5% to 3% is worth more than doubling new customer acquisition. Aiprosol's approach: weekly customer-health monitoring, 60-day-out renewal Slack pings to CSM, retention plays automated via the CCO agent.

definition: Lead routing is the rule-based or AI-augmented decision tree that determines which sales rep, which queue, or which automation handles each inbound lead. Modern routing branches on: score, geography, language, ICP fit, AE availability, and account context.
longExplanation: The Aiprosol routing tree: score ≥ 85 → Hot SDR Slack with @AE-on-rota + 5-min SLA. 65-84 → Warm SDR queue with 4-hour SLA. 40-64 → Nurture (auto-sequence). 0-39 → Future archive (quarterly re-score). Routing rules are tuned monthly using closed-won data. Implemented as an n8n branching workflow.

definition: Your tool stack is the set of SaaS apps your business operates on. The AI-era stack typically includes: LLM, workflow orchestrator (Zapier / Make / n8n), CRM (HubSpot / Pipedrive), email (Resend / Mailgun), calendar (Cal / Calendly), analytics (PostHog), support (Plain / Intercom), billing (Stripe).
longExplanation: Aiprosol's recommended SMB stack (covered in the $197 AI Tools Stack Starter Kit): a frontier LLM + a budget LLM + n8n self-hosted + HubSpot Free + Supabase pgvector + Resend + Cal.com + PostHog + Plain + Notion + Google Workspace + Slack + Stripe + Mercury. At 25-person scale, total fixed cost ~$1,000/mo (<0.5% of $200k MRR revenue).

definition: Prompt engineering is the discipline of designing inputs to LLMs that produce reliable, high-quality outputs. Components: system prompt (role + constraints), user prompt (the request + context), examples (few-shot), output format spec (JSON schema, structure), guardrails (what to refuse).
longExplanation: Production prompts are short, structured, and tested across edge cases — not the 1000-word 'mega-prompts' that go viral. Aiprosol's AI Business Prompt Vault ($97) ships 200 production-tested prompts in JSON format with system + user_template + best_model + notes per prompt. Each is tested against multiple frontier LLMs and includes when-to-use guidance.

definition: Tool use (a.k.a. function calling) is the LLM's ability to invoke external functions during generation — e.g. 'search the web', 'query the database', 'send an email'. Critical for AI agents. multiple frontier LLMs both support structured tool use with JSON schemas.
longExplanation: Each Aiprosol AI agent has a set of tools scoped to its role. The COO agent can read agent_log and tasks. The CCO agent can read leads + write to outreach_drafts. The DA agent can read every table. Tool definitions include name, description, JSON-schema parameters, and a return-shape contract. The LLM picks which tool to call, the runtime executes it, and the result feeds back into the next generation step.

definition: Embeddings are numeric vectors (typically 768-1536 dimensions) that represent the semantic meaning of text. Two texts with similar meaning produce similar vectors. Used to power: similarity search, RAG, clustering, semantic classification. Generated by embedding models (leading embedding-model APIs).
longExplanation: If you embed the text 'How do I cancel my subscription?' and the text 'I want to stop paying', their vectors are close in space — semantic similarity, not lexical. This is the magic behind RAG: instead of keyword search, you find the most-similar chunks of your documentation to a user's natural-language question. Storage: vector databases (pgvector, Pinecone, Weaviate).

definition: Idempotency is the property that running an operation multiple times produces the same result as running it once. Critical for webhooks (which often fire multiple times) and any automation that affects state. Implementation: idempotency keys (UUIDs) recognised by downstream APIs.
longExplanation: Stripe webhooks fire 1-3 times per event. Without idempotency, you'd send 3 welcome emails, create 3 CRM records, and credit 3 GL entries. With idempotency, you check 'have I seen this event_id before?' before acting. Tools like Stripe, GitHub, and Slack respect idempotency keys you supply. Aiprosol's Workflow Automation Playbook documents idempotency as one of 7 core production patterns.

definition: A webhook is an HTTP POST sent from one system to another when an event occurs — the modern alternative to polling. Examples: Stripe → your server on every charge, Calendly → your server on every booking, GitHub → your server on every commit.
longExplanation: Webhooks invert the API call direction. Instead of you constantly asking 'any new charges?', the source pushes you the data the moment it happens. Most modern SaaS supports webhooks. Best practice: respond 200 immediately + process async, verify signatures to prevent spoofing, implement idempotency keys for retries.

definition: Customer Relationship Management system — the database of every prospect, customer, deal, and interaction. Aiprosol-recommended SMB picks: HubSpot Free (marketing-heavy teams), Pipedrive (sales-pipeline teams), Attio (modern alternative with better UX).
longExplanation: The CRM is the single source of truth for your revenue motion. Every lead → contact → deal → customer → renewal flows through it. Aiprosol-recommended pairing: HubSpot Free for the CRM + n8n for automation glue + a vector DB for retrieval. Avoid upgrading to HubSpot Pro until $5M+ ARR — the savings buy you a much better AI tool stack.

definition: Agent orchestration is the design of how multiple AI agents coordinate to handle complex workflows — passing tasks, sharing context, escalating to humans. The pattern behind Aiprosol's 10-agent C-suite where each agent has a role and they coordinate via a daily cron + shared state.
longExplanation: Orchestration design choices: (a) hierarchical (one orchestrator agent dispatches to specialist agents — used for Aiprosol's Arora-as-CEO pattern), (b) peer-to-peer (agents communicate directly via shared memory — fragile at scale), (c) pipeline (output of one feeds the next — used for document processing). All three are valid; pick based on workflow shape, latency tolerance, and audit needs.

definition: A system prompt is the initial set of instructions given to an LLM that defines its role, constraints, and behaviour. Distinct from the user prompt (the request) and example outputs (few-shot). The system prompt is the most-leveraged input for steering an LLM in production.
longExplanation: Best practice: short, structured, behaviour-focused. Bad: 1,000-word 'mega-prompts'. Aiprosol's agents each have a system prompt of ~300 words that defines role, allowed tools, output JSON schema, and refusal cases. The system prompt is reusable; only the user prompt changes per request.

definition: Few-shot prompting includes 2-5 input/output examples in the prompt so the LLM learns the pattern by demonstration. Materially improves accuracy on classification, extraction, and format-sensitive tasks vs. zero-shot.
longExplanation: The trade-off: examples consume tokens (cost + latency). Sweet spot is 3-5 diverse examples covering the edge cases. Beyond 5 examples, returns diminish and you're better off fine-tuning a smaller model. Aiprosol's 200-prompt vault uses few-shot patterns for the classification + extraction prompts where format matters.

definition: A token is the unit of text an LLM processes — roughly 0.75 words in English. 'Aiprosol' is 1 token; 'aiprosol.com' is 4 tokens. LLM pricing is per-token; LLM context windows are measured in tokens.
longExplanation: a frontier LLM: 200K-token context window, ~$3/$15 per million tokens (input/output). a budget LLM: 128K tokens, ~$0.15/$0.60. At Aiprosol scale (10 agents × ~2,500 tokens × daily run × 30 days) = ~750K tokens/month, well under most free-tier limits.

definition: The context window is the maximum number of tokens an LLM can process in a single request — the combined system prompt + user prompt + conversation history + retrieved context + output. a frontier LLM: 200K tokens. a frontier LLM: 128K. a frontier LLM: 2M.
longExplanation: Why it matters: long context = more grounding data per request = less hallucination. Aiprosol's RAG over customer playbooks fits ~150 pages in a single frontier-LLM request. For workflows hitting context limits, the pattern is: chunk + retrieve → only feed the most-relevant chunks → maintain a summary in the message thread.

definition: Fine-tuning is training a base LLM on your domain-specific data so it performs better on your tasks without long prompts. Use cases: highly-structured outputs, brand voice, domain-specific classification.
longExplanation: In 2026, fine-tuning is mostly unnecessary for SMBs — frontier models with RAG cover 95% of needs at lower TCO. Fine-tune only when (a) prompts are >2K tokens and you can't shorten them, (b) you need consistent brand-voice outputs in production, or (c) you're running at scale where per-token cost matters more than initial training cost.

definition: Hallucination is when an LLM confidently outputs false information — fabricated citations, made-up statistics, invented quotes. The defining failure mode of LLMs and the primary risk in production deployment.
longExplanation: Mitigations: (1) RAG so the LLM grounds answers in your data, (2) ask for confidence scores + abstention options, (3) human-in-the-loop on customer-facing outputs, (4) structured outputs with verifiable fields, (5) instruct the model to say 'I don't know' explicitly. Aiprosol's workflows never let AI ship a customer-facing email or filing without human review precisely because hallucination risk is non-zero.

definition: Human-in-the-loop (HITL) is the design pattern where AI produces a draft + a human reviews + approves before action. Critical for any workflow where errors have financial, legal, or reputational consequences.
longExplanation: Aiprosol's customer-facing workflows are almost universally HITL: AI drafts replies, lead-gen emails, contract redlines; humans approve before send. The exception is internal triage (categorising tickets, scoring leads) where errors are recoverable. Patterns: Slack approval buttons, /studio review queue, email-with-approve-link.

definition: An approval gate is a workflow step that pauses for human approval before executing the next step. Critical for any automation with financial, legal, or customer-facing consequences. Implemented via Slack interactive buttons, email-with-action-link, or admin dashboard queues.
longExplanation: Pattern: AI drafts → Slack message with Approve/Edit/Reject buttons → on approve, send; on edit, open editor; on reject, archive. Aiprosol's outreach drafts use this pattern. Add a 24h auto-archive so abandoned approvals don't pile up. Track approval rate as a KPI — if approvers are bulk-approving without reading, the gate is theatre.

definition: JSON mode is a parameter that forces an LLM to return valid JSON matching a specified schema. Eliminates the 'paste JSON output' parsing fragility and unlocks structured outputs at scale. Supported by all major and most production LLMs.
longExplanation: Without JSON mode: LLMs occasionally return malformed JSON, miss commas, add explanatory prose. With JSON mode + a Zod / Pydantic schema: outputs are guaranteed structurally valid. Aiprosol's agents all use JSON mode for their `items[]`, `kpis[]`, `alerts[]`, `proposed_tasks[]` outputs.

definition: LLM latency is the time from API request to response. Frontier models: 2-10 seconds typical, longer for long outputs. Affects user experience for chat interfaces; less relevant for batch workflows.
longExplanation: Strategies to reduce: (a) use streaming so the user sees tokens as they generate, (b) use smaller/faster models (a fast open-source LLM ~200ms vs a frontier LLM ~3-5s), (c) cache common queries, (d) parallel-call multiple models and use first response. Aiprosol's chat widget streams tokens; agent workflows tolerate 5-10s per call.

definition: Streaming is the LLM API pattern of sending tokens to the client as they're generated, instead of waiting for the full response. Materially improves perceived latency for chat / drafting use cases.
longExplanation: Without streaming: user clicks submit, sees nothing for 5 seconds, then full response appears. With streaming: tokens stream within 200-500ms. Implementation: server-sent events (SSE) or WebSocket. Aiprosol's chat widget uses SSE streaming for the Arora chat.

definition: MRR expansion is increasing recurring revenue from existing customers — upgrades, add-ons, seat expansion. Healthier than new-logo MRR because acquisition cost is zero and customer trust already established. Best SaaS businesses run 110-130% net dollar retention.
longExplanation: Expansion triggers Aiprosol's CRO agent watches: usage hitting tier limits, accessing higher-tier features, multi-team adoption, payment increase requests. Each trigger fires a CSM Slack alert with an account summary + suggested upgrade pitch. Most expansion happens around 90-180 days after initial signup.

definition: Net Dollar Retention (NDR) = (starting MRR + expansion − contraction − churn) / starting MRR × 100. Measures revenue health of an existing customer cohort. Above 100% means customers grow faster than they leave; below 100% means leaky bucket.
longExplanation: Industry benchmarks: 110% = healthy SaaS, 120% = great, 130%+ = best-in-class (Snowflake, Datadog). For consultancy / managed services like Aiprosol, NDR is measured the same way — managed-plan customers either expand (more workflows, more users) or contract (downgrade tier, fewer workflows).

definition: ICP is the named segment of customers your product serves best — defined by industry, size, revenue, role, geography, and any other firmographic + technographic signals. The single most-important sales discipline: ICP-shaped outreach converts; spray-and-pray doesn't.
longExplanation: Aiprosol's ICP: SMBs 10-500 employees, $1M-$50M ARR, English-language ops, in our top-7 industries (Legal, Real Estate, Manufacturing, Retail, Financial Services, E-commerce, SaaS, Professional Services). The 4-component lead scoring model is calibrated to this ICP — leads outside it score under 40 by design.

definition: PQL is a lead that's shown product behaviour indicating buying intent — used a key feature, hit a paywall, invited teammates, exceeded free-tier limits. PQL converts 5-10x better than MQL (Marketing-Qualified Lead) because behaviour is a stronger signal than form-fill.
longExplanation: PQL scoring needs product usage data + a scoring model. Tools: Endgame, Pocus, MadKudu, or a custom model. For SaaS with self-serve onboarding, PQL is the highest-converting lead source. Aiprosol's Lead Generation Playbook covers PQL scoring as part of the 4-component model.

definition: A drip campaign is an automated multi-touch email sequence — 3-12 emails sent over days or weeks. Most common shapes: welcome series, lead nurture, re-engagement, post-purchase, win-back. The 5-touch Aiprosol nurture sequence is a typical example.
longExplanation: Best practice: each touch earns the next read by providing standalone value, not just 'following up'. Implementation: Customer.io, Loops, Resend + scheduled jobs, or n8n state-machine workflow. Aiprosol typically deploys drip campaigns inside Customer.io or Loops; for low-volume work, n8n with persisted state suffices.

definition: Webhook signature verification is the security pattern of cryptographically verifying that an incoming webhook actually came from the claimed source (Stripe, GitHub, Slack). Without it, anyone can spoof your webhook endpoint and trigger arbitrary actions.
longExplanation: Pattern: provider signs the payload with a shared secret + HMAC-SHA256; you re-compute the signature on receipt + compare. Stripe, GitHub, Slack, Calendly all support this. Always verify before acting on payload data, even in 'private' endpoints — webhook URLs leak through deployment logs, screenshots, and CI configs.

definition: A cron job is a scheduled task that runs at fixed times or intervals — every hour, every day at 9am UTC, every Monday. The backbone of polling workflows and scheduled batch operations. Aiprosol's 10 AI agents run on a daily 9am UTC cron.
longExplanation: Cron syntax: `0 9 * * *` = every day at 9am. `*/15 * * * *` = every 15 minutes. Implementation: Vercel cron, GitHub Actions schedule, Cloudflare Workers, or n8n's scheduleTrigger node. Best practice: each cron job has a failure alert, success log, and exclusive-execution lock (so overlapping runs don't double-fire).

definition: An idempotency key is a unique identifier (typically UUID) you supply with API requests so the downstream system recognises duplicates and safely de-duplicates. Stripe, GitHub, Slack, AWS all accept idempotency keys.
longExplanation: Why it matters: webhooks fire 1-3 times per event. Without idempotency, you create 3 customers / send 3 emails / charge 3 cards. With idempotency key per logical event: downstream API recognises the duplicate and returns the original response without re-executing. Aiprosol's Workflow Automation Playbook covers idempotency as one of 14 patterns.

definition: Sales SLA (Service Level Agreement) is the internal commitment to respond to inbound leads within a set time — typically 5 minutes for hot leads, 4 hours for warm, end-of-day for cold. Measured and reported; misses trigger escalation.
longExplanation: Aiprosol's SLA architecture: hot leads (score ≥ 85) ping AE Slack with a 5-min SLA. If no AE response in 10 min, escalate to manager Slack. If no manager response in 20 min, escalate to founder. Reps see a dashboard with their personal SLA hit-rate weekly. Reps under 85% trigger 1:1 coaching.

definition: A knowledge base is the structured collection of documentation, SOPs, playbooks, and FAQs a team operates from. Modern KBs are RAG-indexed so AI can answer questions from them directly. Tools: Notion, Confluence, Mintlify, custom.
longExplanation: For Aiprosol customers, we typically build the KB inside Notion (organisation-friendly) + index it via Supabase pgvector for RAG. AI agents (especially CCO for customer support) query the KB before drafting responses, citing specific KB pages. The KB is the single source of truth — agent answers grounded in it are auditable.

definition: CSAT is the % of customers who rate their experience as 'satisfied' or 'very satisfied' after an interaction — typically support tickets, calls, or onboarding. Measured post-interaction with a 1-5 or 1-10 scale.
longExplanation: Industry benchmark: 80%+ is healthy for SaaS / managed services. Below 70% is a problem. CSAT for support tickets is the leading indicator of churn — sustained low CSAT predicts customer departure 90-180 days out. Aiprosol's CCO agent flags any customer with CSAT < 7 for retention play within 24 hours.

definition: NPS is a 0-100 scoring system measuring customer loyalty. Calculation: % of customers rating 9-10 (Promoters) − % rating 0-6 (Detractors), ignoring 7-8 (Passives). Above 30 is good, above 50 is excellent.
longExplanation: NPS is lagging — it tells you what happened. Pair with leading indicators (usage trend, support sentiment, payment timing) for early-warning. Aiprosol surveys customers quarterly with a single 0-10 question + open-ended follow-up. Promoters get asked for referrals + reviews; Detractors get a CSM call within 7 days.

definition: API rate limit is the maximum number of requests an API accepts per time window. Exceeding it returns HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests). Production workflows MUST handle rate limits gracefully — retry with exponential backoff, or queue + throttle.
longExplanation: Common rate limits: Stripe 100 req/s, leading LLM API 500 req/min, Twilio 1 req/s, Gmail 250 quota units/s. Rule of thumb: throttle to 80% of the documented limit so bursts don't fail. n8n's HTTP node has built-in retry; Zapier's needs explicit error handlers; Make handles via the Sleep module.

definition: Exponential backoff is the retry pattern where wait time doubles between failed attempts — 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, 16s. Used for transient failures (rate limits, network errors). Prevents thundering-herd amplification of a broken upstream.
longExplanation: Pattern: catch retryable error → wait 2^attempt seconds → retry up to N times → fail permanently if N exceeded. Add jitter (random 0-1s) so concurrent retries don't synchronise. Aiprosol's agent runner uses exponential backoff for LLM 429 (rate limit) responses.

definition: A dead letter queue is the storage for messages that failed processing after all retries exhausted. Lets you inspect, debug, and replay failures without blocking the main queue. Essential for any async workflow processing real volume.
longExplanation: Tools: AWS SQS native DLQ, Inngest Functions, Trigger.dev, Hatchet. For SMB scale with n8n: a Supabase table named workflow_failures storing {workflow, payload, error, attempts, last_attempted_at}. The /studio Tasks tab pulls from this table for human review.

definition: Schema evolution is the discipline of changing a data structure (database table, API contract, JSON shape) without breaking existing consumers. Add fields, never remove. Default values for new fields. Versioned contracts when breaking changes are unavoidable.
longExplanation: In LLM-based systems, schema evolution especially matters for the JSON-mode outputs. If you add a field to the agent's output schema, every old log entry needs to be re-readable. Strategy: every schema change includes a migration that upgrades existing records to the new shape with sensible defaults.

definition: Multi-tenancy is a SaaS architecture where one codebase + database serves many isolated customer accounts. Tenant data isolation is enforced via tenant_id columns + row-level security policies. Critical for B2B SaaS pricing/scaling economics.
longExplanation: Aiprosol's /studio (when extended to host customer ops, future plan) will be multi-tenant via Supabase RLS — each customer's tasks/leads/drafts isolated to their tenant_id. Single-tenant is much simpler operationally; multi-tenant is much cheaper at scale. Most consultancies stay single-tenant per customer for the first 5-10 engagements.

definition: Zero-Data-Retention is an enterprise LLM tier where the vendor commits to not retaining your prompts or responses for training. Available on a frontier LLM (enterprise tier), an enterprise LLM tier, Azure-hosted LLM. Required for processing PII, PHI, or contract-sensitive data.
longExplanation: Standard API tiers may retain prompts/responses for 30 days for abuse monitoring. ZDR tiers don't retain at all. Important for: legal (privilege), finance (regulated data), healthcare (HIPAA), enterprise contracts with data-residency clauses. Cost is roughly the same per-token; gate is contractual + sometimes minimum-spend.

definition: Compliance posture is your organisation's overall regulatory readiness — controls in place, audits passed, certifications held. Common SMB-relevant frameworks: SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA (healthcare), PCI DSS (payment cards).
longExplanation: For B2B SaaS / managed services in 2026, SOC 2 Type II is essentially table stakes for enterprise sales. Tools that streamline: Vanta, Drata, Secureframe. Cost: $5-15K/year platform + $10-25K audit annually. Aiprosol's Enterprise plan architecture is SOC 2 compliant; the certification is on roadmap once we have the first 3 paying enterprise customers.

definition: GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) is the EU's privacy framework — consent for processing, right to deletion, data portability, DPA contracts, 72-hour breach notification. Applies to any business processing EU residents' data, regardless of where the business is based.
longExplanation: GDPR-relevant moves for any SaaS: cookie consent banner, privacy policy with explicit lawful basis, Data Processing Agreement signed with every data sub-processor (LLM vendors, email APIs, analytics), right-to-deletion endpoint, breach incident playbook. Aiprosol's /privacy and /cookies pages cover this; tooling: Cookiebot, OneTrust at enterprise scale, or hand-rolled CookieBanner component at SMB scale.

definition: CCPA (California Consumer Privacy Act) is California's privacy framework. Similar in spirit to GDPR — right to know, right to delete, right to opt out of sale. Applies to businesses serving California residents with $25M+ revenue OR 50K+ consumers OR 50% revenue from selling personal info.
longExplanation: Practical SMB implications: same as GDPR — privacy policy, DPA contracts, deletion endpoint. Plus a 'Do Not Sell My Personal Information' link on your site if you sell user data (most B2B SaaS don't, so this is optional). Compliance overhead is mostly identical to GDPR — implement once for both.

definition: SOC 2 is the security audit framework most B2B SaaS customers require. Five trust criteria: Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy. Two report types: Type I (point-in-time), Type II (over 6-12 months). Type II is the enterprise standard.
longExplanation: Cost: $10-25K/year for audit + $5-15K/year for compliance platform (Vanta/Drata/Secureframe). Timeline: 4-6 months to first Type II. Required for: enterprise B2B SaaS sales >$50K ACV. Aiprosol's Enterprise plan architecture is SOC 2 ready; certification on roadmap.

definition: ISO 27001 is the international information security management standard. More documentation-heavy than SOC 2. Required for: European enterprise sales, government contracts, financial services in some jurisdictions.
longExplanation: Most US-based B2B SaaS prioritise SOC 2; UK + EU customers often require ISO 27001. Cost: similar to SOC 2 ($15-30K all-in annually). Tools: Vanta, Drata, ISMS.online. The two certifications overlap ~70%; getting both costs maybe 1.4x getting one.

definition: A PIA (sometimes DPIA — Data Protection Impact Assessment) is a structured analysis of how a project handles personal data and what risks it creates. Required under GDPR for high-risk processing (AI decisions, large-scale data, special categories).
longExplanation: For AI automation projects: GDPR Article 35 requires a DPIA if you're using AI to make decisions affecting individuals (lead scoring, automated triage). Aiprosol provides a template DPIA in the Enterprise Kit ($397). For SMB scale, the template + 2 hours of legal review is usually sufficient. Don't skip — privacy regulator fines for missed DPIAs run €10K-100K typical.

definition: CAC is the total cost of acquiring one new paying customer — paid ads + content + sales rep time + tooling, divided by customers acquired in the same period. Pair with LTV for unit-economics health.
longExplanation: Industry benchmark: LTV:CAC ratio of 3:1 is healthy SaaS, 5:1 is great, <1:1 is broken. For Aiprosol's Starter plan ($997/mo with 24-month median retention): LTV ~$23,928. Healthy CAC ceiling: $8,000. For Enterprise ($7,997/mo): LTV ~$192K, CAC ceiling ~$64K. These ceilings inform what's reasonable to spend on customer acquisition.

definition: LTV is the total revenue from a customer over their entire relationship. Calculation for subscriptions: ARPU × Gross Margin × (1 / Monthly Churn Rate). For Aiprosol Starter at $997/mo, 80% GM, 4% monthly churn: LTV = $997 × 0.8 × 25 = $19,940.
longExplanation: LTV is the most-leveraged SaaS number — small changes to churn or expansion compound dramatically. Reducing churn from 5% to 3% increases LTV by 67%. LTV calculations get more accurate with cohort data; for early-stage businesses, model 3-5 scenarios and use the median for planning. Industry tools: ChartMogul, Mosaic, or a custom Looker / Mode dashboard.

definition: ACV is the annualised revenue from a single contract. For monthly subscriptions: ACV = monthly fee × 12. For multi-year contracts: total contract value / years. Pair with ARR (sum of all ACVs) for company-level growth.
longExplanation: ACV bands SaaS companies typically segment by: < $5K (SMB self-serve), $5-25K (mid-market), $25-100K (enterprise), $100K+ (strategic). Aiprosol's Enterprise plan at $7,997/mo = $96K ACV puts customers in solid mid-market. Sales motion, pricing, and contract terms differ materially across these bands.

definition: ARR is the annualised value of all active recurring contracts. ARR = MRR × 12 for monthly subscriptions. The headline number reported to investors, used for valuations + growth-rate calculations.
longExplanation: ARR is the SaaS valuation anchor — companies are typically valued at 5-15x ARR depending on growth rate and gross margin. Don't conflate ARR with revenue — ARR is forward-looking (what you'd collect over the next 12 months at current MRR), revenue is backward-looking (what you've actually collected).

definition: A churn cohort is the group of customers that signed up in the same month (or quarter), tracked over time to measure how many remain. Healthier than aggregate churn because it controls for seasonality and product-version differences.
longExplanation: Healthy SaaS cohort curves: 90%+ retained at month 3, 80%+ at month 12, plateau above 70% by month 24. Aggressive declines in early months → onboarding problem. Steady decline throughout → product fit issue. Aiprosol's CCO agent monitors retention cohorts weekly; >5% month-over-month decline triggers an exec Slack alert.

definition: Retention is the % of customers (or revenue) you keep over a defined period. Inverse of churn. Two flavours: logo retention (customer count) and gross dollar retention (revenue, excluding expansion).
longExplanation: Healthy SaaS: 90%+ gross dollar retention. Add expansion (upsells, seat adds) and you get net dollar retention — 110%+ is great, 120%+ is best-in-class. Retention is the single biggest lever in SaaS economics; a 5% churn rate vs 3% changes valuation by 60%+.

definition: Onboarding is the structured first 30-90 days of a new customer relationship — getting them set up, integrated, and producing measurable value. Most-leveraged moment for reducing churn — 80% of cancellations correlate with poor onboarding.
longExplanation: Aiprosol's Phase 0 (Days 0-14) is the onboarding for managed plans: SSO + DPA + audit + pilot users + baselines + first-workflow-live. Customers who hit Phase 0 milestones on schedule are 4x more likely to renew at month 12. The CCO agent monitors onboarding milestone hit-rate as a leading retention indicator.

definition: Activation rate is the % of signups who reach a defined 'aha moment' (key action) within a defined window. Used in product-led growth. Better leading indicator than signup rate.
longExplanation: Aiprosol-style activation for managed plans: customer ships their first workflow within 14 days of signing. PLG-style activation: trial user uses the product ≥3 times in week 1. Activation rate × Conversion rate × LTV is the unit economics equation for product-led businesses. Most SaaS companies have <30% activation rate; getting it to 60%+ is the highest-leverage growth lever.

definition: Time-to-value (TTV) is how long from sign-up to the customer experiencing the first concrete benefit. For Aiprosol managed plans, TTV target is 14 days (Phase 0 → first workflow live). Critical onboarding metric.
longExplanation: Shorter TTV correlates strongly with retention — customers who get value fast trust the system + invest more. Strategies to compress TTV: pre-built templates (so the customer doesn't start from blank), high-touch onboarding for the first 14 days, instrumented dashboards so progress is visible. Aiprosol's Starter plan delivers Phase 0 in 14 days; Growth and Enterprise have similar promise.

definition: AI safety is the discipline of preventing AI systems from causing harm — through deception, misuse, hallucination, or unintended consequences. For business automation: input validation, output filters, audit logs, kill switches, human-in-the-loop for high-stakes decisions.
longExplanation: Practical AI safety for SMB automation: (1) never give AI the final word on customer-facing output without human approval, (2) never give AI the ability to spend money or send legal communications without human approval, (3) log every AI action with full reproducibility, (4) implement a kill switch that disables all AI workflows in one command if something goes wrong. Aiprosol's architecture follows all four.

definition: Prompt injection is the attack where malicious input data tricks an LLM into ignoring its system prompt and following the attacker's instructions. The defining security risk for AI agents that process user-supplied content (emails, support tickets, documents).
longExplanation: Mitigations: (1) treat all user-supplied content as data, never as instructions, (2) use system-prompt-only auth (the LLM trusts system messages, not embedded user text), (3) output validation — reject responses that contain trigger phrases like 'IGNORE PREVIOUS INSTRUCTIONS', (4) human-in-the-loop on any AI output that affects state. Aiprosol's agents have explicit prompt-injection defence in their system prompts plus output validation layers.

definition: Tool calling reliability is the consistency with which an LLM correctly invokes external functions with valid parameters — vs. malformed JSON, wrong tool, hallucinated parameters. Frontier models (a frontier LLM, a frontier LLM) hit 95-98% reliability; older models below 80%.
longExplanation: Strategies to improve reliability: (1) clear tool descriptions with examples, (2) structured outputs with JSON schema validation, (3) limit available tools per task to 3-5 (more = confusion), (4) provide few-shot examples of correct tool calls, (5) retry on validation failure with the error message embedded. Aiprosol's agents each have <8 tools available, scoped to their role.

definition: CPA is the marketing cost to drive one defined action — typically a lead form fill, demo booking, or signup. CPA = ad spend / actions. Pair with conversion rate (CPA / signup-to-paying conversion) to get full CAC.
longExplanation: Industry benchmarks for B2B SaaS in 2026: $50-200 CPA on a free trial / demo request, $5-20 on a content download. CPA varies hugely by channel — paid search $80-150, LinkedIn ads $200-400, content/SEO 'free' but with 6-12 month payback. Aiprosol's primary channels target $50-100 CPA (ROI audit form fills).

definition: CRO is the discipline of increasing the % of visitors who take a desired action — fill a form, book a call, buy. A/B testing, copy improvement, friction reduction, social proof. Compounds with traffic — 2% → 3% conversion is a 50% revenue lift at constant spend.
longExplanation: Top SMB CRO levers: (1) hero copy that names the prospect's exact pain in 8 words, (2) single primary CTA (not three), (3) social proof above the fold (logos, numbers, testimonials), (4) friction reduction in forms (fewer fields, smart defaults), (5) page speed (every 100ms = ~1% conversion impact). Aiprosol's homepage hero is heavily CRO-tuned.

definition: Attribution model is how you assign credit for a conversion across multiple marketing touchpoints. Common models: first-touch, last-touch, linear, time-decay, position-based. Different models tell different stories about which channels work.
longExplanation: Reality: no attribution model is fully correct (B2B buyer journeys take 5-50 touchpoints over 6-18 months). Use multiple models in parallel. Aiprosol-style: time-decay attribution for paid channels + first-touch + last-touch reported for context. Tools: PostHog Funnels, Segment + custom, or Demandbase / Bombora for ABM.

definition: Bounce rate is the % of visitors who land on a page and leave without interacting. High bounce rate (>70%) signals mismatch between traffic source and page content. Healthy bounce: 40-55% for content pages, 20-40% for landing pages.
longExplanation: Don't optimise bounce rate in isolation — a high bounce on a glossary page might be perfectly healthy (visitor got their answer + left). Optimise alongside time-on-page + scroll depth. Tools: PostHog, Plausible, GA4. Aiprosol uses PostHog with 'time to first scroll' as the bounce-quality signal.

definition: Page authority is a relative score (typically 1-100, vendor-specific) measuring a single URL's SEO strength. Pair with Domain Authority (full-site) for the SEO health picture. Tools: Moz, Ahrefs, Semrush each compute their own version.
longExplanation: Page authority isn't an official Google signal — it's a third-party proxy. Real SEO ranking factors include: backlinks (the strongest), content depth + relevance, freshness, click-through rate from SERP, dwell time, page speed. Aiprosol's GEO strategy bets on the answer-engine era where AI engines weight structured schema, citation, and primary-source content above traditional link metrics.

definition: GEO is the discipline of making content visible in AI answer engines — AI answer engines, Google AI Overviews. Different signals than classic SEO. Cite-worthy content, structured schema, opinion + primary-source data, llms.txt files all matter.
longExplanation: Top GEO levers in 2026: (1) invite AI crawlers in robots.txt, (2) publish llms.txt with curated guide, (3) Schema.org markup (Article, FAQPage, HowTo, DefinedTerm, Person, Organization), (4) original primary-source content (research, comparisons, definitions), (5) get cited in AI training data via directory submissions + GitHub awesome lists, (6) submit URLs via IndexNow for instant Bing/Yandex crawl. Aiprosol's GEO playbook is documented end-to-end at /how-to and across the structured content layer.

definition: SEO is the discipline of making content rank in classic search engines (Google, Bing). Key factors: backlinks from authority sites, content depth + relevance, page speed, structured data, mobile-friendliness, click-through rate from SERP, dwell time.
longExplanation: SEO matters in 2026 but is in transition: Google AI Overviews are intercepting 30%+ of high-intent queries before the user reaches the blue links. So you optimise for both classic SEO + GEO. Practically: keyword-relevant H1/H2, internal linking topology, Schema.org markup, fast core web vitals, and content that genuinely answers the searcher's intent (not just hits the keyword).

definition: A backlink is another website linking TO your page. The strongest classic-SEO ranking signal — Google's PageRank algorithm was built on backlink graph analysis. In 2026, quality > quantity: 5 high-authority backlinks beat 500 low-authority ones.
longExplanation: Earning backlinks in 2026: (1) publish original primary-source research that journalists + LLM-engine crawlers cite, (2) submit to high-authority directories (Crunchbase, AI-tool databases), (3) guest posts on respected industry publications, (4) podcast appearances (the show notes link back), (5) get on GitHub awesome lists. Aiprosol's GEO playbook covers each.

definition: Internal linking is the practice of linking from one page on your site to another. Strengthens topical authority + helps crawlers (human + AI) navigate your content. Aiprosol's glossary entries all link to related terms; comparison pages link to related comparisons; how-to pages link to related products.
longExplanation: Internal linking strategy: (1) identify pillar pages (your most important pages) + ensure many other pages link to them, (2) use descriptive anchor text ("AI workflow audit framework" not "click here"), (3) link from high-traffic pages to high-conversion pages, (4) don't over-link — 3-8 internal links per page is the sweet spot. Aiprosol's structured content layer is heavily internal-linked.

definition: Anchor text is the visible, clickable text in a hyperlink. Affects SEO (signals what the linked page is about) + accessibility (screen readers announce it). Best practice: descriptive, keyword-relevant, not generic ("click here" is the canonical bad example).
longExplanation: Optimisation: keyword-relevant for SEO, action-oriented for conversion, descriptive for accessibility. Avoid: "click here", "read more", "this page". Use: "read the workflow automation playbook", "download the ROI calculator", "view the AI tools comparison". Aiprosol's link copy follows this consistently.

definition: A featured snippet is the answer box at the top of Google's SERP — typically pulled from a page that ranks 2-10. Drives 35-60% of all clicks for the queries where it appears. Optimised for by structuring content as direct question-answer pairs.
longExplanation: How to win featured snippets: (1) H2 = the question ("How do I score inbound leads?"), (2) immediately under the H2, a 40-60 word direct answer in a paragraph or list, (3) more detail follows. Don't bury the lead. FAQPage schema also helps. Aiprosol's how-to pages + FAQ pages are explicitly structured for this.

definition: AI Overview is Google's AI-generated answer at the top of SERP. Intercepts the click for many informational queries. Launched 2024, expanded 2025. Citation source URLs are listed, driving meaningful traffic to cited pages.
longExplanation: Optimisation for AI Overview citation: (1) structured data (FAQPage, HowTo, DefinedTerm), (2) clear H2/H3 structure with direct answers, (3) primary-source content (not aggregation), (4) author + organisation E-E-A-T signals, (5) recency — Google preferentially cites recently-updated content. Aiprosol's GEO foundation is built for this.

definition: Domain authority is a third-party score (1-100, vendor-specific) measuring a domain's overall SEO strength. Computed from backlink profile + content + technical health. Tools: Moz DA, Ahrefs DR, Semrush AS. Not an official Google signal, but correlates with rankings.
longExplanation: New domains start at DA ~10. Building to DA 40+ takes 12-24 months of consistent quality content + backlink earning. Aiprosol's aiprosol.com is a fresh 2026 domain at low DA; the GEO strategy aims to bypass DA dependency via structured content + AI engine citation rather than waiting years for classic backlink accumulation.

definition: A canonical URL is the preferred version of a page when duplicate or near-duplicate versions exist (e.g. with/without trailing slash, query strings, mobile/desktop). Declared via `<link rel="canonical" href="...">` in the page head. Prevents SEO dilution.
longExplanation: Common duplicate scenarios that need canonicals: tracking URLs (?utm_source=...), pagination, AMP versions, mobile subdomain (m.example.com), HTTP vs HTTPS, www vs non-www. Aiprosol declares canonical on every page via Next.js metadata.alternates.canonical — points to the bare URL without query parameters.

definition: Open Graph (og:*) is the meta-tag protocol that controls how a URL appears when shared on social platforms (Facebook, LinkedIn, Slack, Discord). Title, description, image (1200x630), URL, type. Critical for share CTR.
longExplanation: Required tags: og:title (≤60 chars), og:description (≤155 chars), og:image (1200x630, ≤8MB), og:url (canonical), og:type (article/website/product). Twitter Cards (twitter:*) are a parallel protocol; modern best practice is to use og:* + opt-in twitter:card. Aiprosol generates per-page OG images via /api/og/[type]/[slug] route handlers.

definition: Core Web Vitals are Google's three page-performance signals: LCP (Largest Contentful Paint, target <2.5s), INP (Interaction to Next Paint, target <200ms), CLS (Cumulative Layout Shift, target <0.1). Direct ranking factor since 2021.
longExplanation: Practical optimisation: (1) optimise hero image (largest LCP element) — webp/avif format, proper sizing, priority loading; (2) defer non-critical JS (the biggest INP killer); (3) reserve space for ads/images so they don't shift layout (CLS). Tools: PageSpeed Insights, Chrome User Experience Report, Vercel Analytics. Aiprosol targets 90+ on all three desktop + 75+ on mobile.

definition: LCP measures when the largest visible element on a page finishes rendering. Target: under 2.5 seconds. The element is usually the hero image, hero headline, or a video. Critical Core Web Vital.
longExplanation: Fixes for slow LCP: (1) preload the hero image (`<link rel="preload" as="image">`), (2) serve next-gen formats (webp, avif), (3) size images correctly (no 4000px image for a 800px viewport), (4) avoid hero render-blocking on font loading. Aiprosol's hero uses Three.js for the AI constellation; the LCP is the cycling-headline H1 text, which is fast.

definition: INP measures the responsiveness of all user interactions on a page (clicks, taps, key presses). Target: under 200ms p75. Replaced FID in 2024 as a Core Web Vital. The most common cause of poor INP: large JS bundles blocking the main thread.
longExplanation: Fixes for poor INP: (1) code-split heavy components (lazy-load, dynamic imports), (2) defer non-critical scripts to after first interaction, (3) use Web Workers for CPU-bound work, (4) avoid heavy useEffect on mount. React 19's Server Components help materially. Aiprosol's bundle is 102 KB shared chunk — already lean.

definition: CLS measures unexpected layout movement during page load — content jumping as images load, ads appear, fonts swap. Target: under 0.1. High CLS = bad UX + ranking penalty.
longExplanation: Fixes for poor CLS: (1) declare width + height on all images so the browser reserves space, (2) reserve space for ads + embeds, (3) use font-display: optional or swap with size-adjust to prevent FOUC, (4) avoid inserting content above existing content after page load. Aiprosol's design system uses fixed aspect-ratios on all images.

definition: Alt text is the text alternative for an image, declared via the `alt` attribute. Used by screen readers (accessibility), search engines (SEO + AI training), and when the image fails to load. Required for accessibility compliance (WCAG 2.1 AA).
longExplanation: Best practice: describe the image's content + function. Decorative images: `alt=""` (empty, not omitted). Informative images: short factual description ("chart showing 35h/week reclaimed across 4 functions"). Functional images (buttons): describe the action ("Submit form"). Aiprosol's image audit requires alt text on every non-decorative image.

definition: Accessibility (a11y) is designing + building so that people with disabilities can use your product. Encompasses: screen reader support, keyboard navigation, sufficient colour contrast, captions on video, alt text on images, semantic HTML, focus management.
longExplanation: Standard: WCAG 2.1 AA. Practical impact: 15-20% of users have some form of disability that affects web use. Beyond ethics + legal requirements (ADA, EU Accessibility Act), accessible sites rank better in search + convert better on average. Tools: axe-core (testing), WAVE (browser extension), Lighthouse a11y audit.

definition: Semantic HTML uses element names that describe content meaning rather than visual styling — <article>, <nav>, <main>, <section>, <aside>, <header>, <footer>, <button>, <time>. Critical for accessibility (screen readers) + SEO (machine understanding).
longExplanation:
Wrong:
click me
(not focusable, not announceable). Right: . Wrong: Title. Right:

Title

. Modern frameworks (Next.js, React 19) make semantic HTML easy if you remember to use the right elements. Aiprosol's components default to semantic.

definition: WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) is the international standard for web accessibility. WCAG 2.1 AA is the typical compliance target — required by law in many jurisdictions (US ADA, EU Accessibility Act, UK Equality Act).
longExplanation: Four principles (POUR): Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust. Practical checks: 4.5:1 colour contrast, alt text on images, keyboard navigation works, focus indicators visible, language declared, form labels associated, error messages clear. WCAG 2.2 (2024) adds requirements around dragging gestures + focus appearance.
